note_data.title: Electrical quantities
keyPoints: Electric charge is measured in coulombs and can be positive or negative
Opposite charges experience an attractive force, like charges experience a repulsive force
An electric field is the region in which an electric charge experiences attractive or repulsive forces
Conductors allow electric charge to flow through them because they have charged particles that are free to move
Insulators impede the flow of electric charge because they don't have charged particles that are free to move
Objects can be charged by transfer of electrons through their addition or removal
Insulators can be electrostatically charged by friction when two insulators are rubbed together
When insulators are rubbed together, electrons move from one to the other making them oppositely charged

definitions.definition: Measured in coulombs and can be positive or negative
The region in which an electric charge experiences attractive or repulsive forces
Materials which allow electric charge to flow through them because they have charged particles that are free to move
Materials which impede the flow of electric charge because they don't have charged particles that are free to move
Electric charge at rest on the surface of charged insulators

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show working clearly with correct formula, substitution and units
State similarities AND differences between the items

modelAnswer.question: A 12V battery is connected to a 4Ω resistor. Calculate (a) the current flowing through the resistor (b) the power dissipated by the resistor.
modelAnswer.answer: (a) Using V = IR, I = V/R = 12V ÷ 4Ω = 3A (b) Using P = I²R, P = (3A)² × 4Ω = 9 × 4 = 36W
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Correct formula P = I²R or P = VI

commonMistakes: Confusing which charges attract and repel - remember opposite charges attract, like charges repel
Thinking that both positive and negative charges move during charging - only electrons (negative charge) transfer
Assuming conductors and insulators work the same way for charge flow
Forgetting that charging by friction makes objects oppositely charged, not the same charge
examinerTips: Remember that metals have free electrons as charge carriers while molten ionic substances have ions as charge carriers
When describing charging by friction, always mention electron transfer specifically and state which object becomes positively and negatively charged
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Circuit diagram showing battery, resistor, ammeter (in series) and voltmeter (in parallel across resistor) with correct circuit symbols and current direction marked
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to calculate current, voltage and resistance in a circuit with two resistors in parallel and explain the relationship between charge and current
N 2022 P1 Q12 (1 mark) tested the definition of potential difference with a multiple choice question about energy per unit charge
M/J 2022 P2 Q3b (4 marks) required students to calculate the charge that flows through a lamp in 5 minutes given the current, and explain why current decreases as temperature increases
N 2023 P2 Q5 (8 marks) combined electrical quantities with circuit analysis, asking for current calculations and power dissipation in a series-parallel combination circuit

workedExample.question: A 12V car battery supplies a current of 8.0A to the headlights for 2.5 hours. Calculate (a) the charge that flows through the circuit (b) the energy transferred by the battery. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Convert time to seconds: t = 2.5 × 60 × 60 = 9000s
Step 2: Calculate charge using Q = It: Q = 8.0 × 9000 = 72,000C
Step 3: Calculate energy using W = VIt or W = VQ: W = 12 × 8.0 × 9000 = 864,000J
Step 4: Convert to more appropriate units: W = 864,000J = 864kJ
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for time conversion, 1 mark for correct formula Q = It, 1 mark for charge calculation, 1 mark for correct formula W = VIt, 1 mark for energy calculation, 1 mark for appropriate units

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused current and charge' — always remember that current is the rate of flow of charge (I = Q/t), not the total amount of charge

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently omitted units or used incorrect units' — always include correct units: amperes (A) for current, volts (V) for potential difference, coulombs (C) for charge, and joules (J) for energy
Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates often failed to rearrange formulas correctly' — practice rearranging V = IR, Q = It, and W = VIt before the exam, and always substitute values after rearranging
note_data.keyDiagram: Circuit diagram showing a battery (two parallel lines, one longer than the other) connected to a resistor (rectangular box or zigzag line) with an ammeter (circle with 'A') in series and a voltmeter (circle with 'V') in parallel across the resistor. Include arrows showing conventional current direction (positive to negative terminal) and label all components clearly with their symbols.
note_data.quickRecap: Electric charge is measured in coulombs and creates forces between charged objects - opposite charges attract while like charges repel. Conductors allow charge flow due to free charge carriers, while insulators impede charge flow. Objects can be charged by electron transfer, particularly through friction between insulators.
